question_frequency.rationale: The Pair posed a clear A/B choice at each of the three main design decisions (new vs in-place, finite vs toroidal, ANSI vs os.system). That's consistent didactic engagement at every meaningful fork, though limited to binary choices rather than open questions.

question_quality.rationale: The first two forks (simultaneous update, boundary handling) hit genuine cruxes of Game of Life. The third (clear screen) is more cosmetic/peripheral. Missing forks include neighbor iteration style and pretty-print encoding.

scaffolding.rationale: Decisions are sequenced logically from core correctness (simultaneous update) to semantics (boundaries) to presentation (clearing). Difficulty descends smoothly. The diff-style options reveal the implementation, which is mildly spoiler-ish but appropriate for a junior engineer comparing approaches.

flow_and_tone.rationale: Tone is warm and affirming ('you nailed the reasoning'), the user's reasoning is acknowledged substantively, and the back-and-forth is fluid. Slightly mechanical due to the repeated A/B template, but conversational overall.

effectiveness.rationale: Final code correctly implements Game of Life with a working glider animation, comparable to baseline. Baseline included both blinker and glider demos plus richer pretty-printing; experiment only does glider. Functionally equivalent for the core task.

friction.rationale: Completed in 4 turns / 124s versus baseline's 5 turns / 212s — tighter than baseline while delivering meaningful pedagogical engagement at each design fork. Good efficiency-to-teaching ratio.
